jokes.text: Applesauce. No, no. L... I'm kidding. It says 'applause.'
jokes.setup: Reading a cue card that apparently says 'applesauce'
jokes.punchline: Reveals it actually says 'applause' - classic warm-up comedy bit
jokes.explanation: Standard warm-up comedian material - functional but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Warm-up Comedian
dialogue.line: Applesauce. No, no. L... I'm kidding. It says 'applause.'

jokes.text: Celebrity caning victim Michael fay... what... what are you giggling at? Every time a guy gets smacked in the ass you laugh?
jokes.setup: Audience giggles at mention of Michael Fay caning story
jokes.punchline: Larry calls out the audience's reaction as juvenile
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry - making the audience uncomfortable by calling out their reaction, meta-comedy that fits his character

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Celebrity caning victim Michael fay... what... what are you giggling at? Every time a guy gets smacked in the ass you laugh?

jokes.text: Something we all have what left over from when we got beaten as a child, I believe.
jokes.setup: Explaining why people laugh at physical punishment
jokes.punchline: Dark psychological explanation about childhood trauma
jokes.explanation: Perfectly neurotic Larry - taking a simple audience reaction and making it deeply psychological and uncomfortable

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Something we all have what left over from when we got beaten as a child, I believe.

jokes.text: I can assure you that is a long flight, especially, uh, when you have to stand the whole way.
jokes.setup: Michael Fay's long flight back from Singapore
jokes.punchline: Can't sit down because his butt was caned
jokes.explanation: Solid setup-punchline construction, predictable but well-executed

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I can assure you that is a long flight, especially, uh, when you have to stand the whole way.

jokes.text: That's... and frankly, I thought that was just common courtesy to shake hands with someone who's seen your butt.
jokes.setup: Michael Fay shook hands with his caner
jokes.punchline: Twisted logic that it's polite to shake hands with someone who's seen your butt
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist logic - takes a news item and finds a completely unexpected angle that's both logical and ridiculous

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That's... and frankly, I thought that was just common courtesy to shake hands with someone who's seen your butt.

jokes.text: You know. Whi... which reminds me. Nice to see you. Woo!
jokes.setup: Just made joke about shaking hands with someone who's seen your butt
jokes.punchline: Implies someone in audience has seen his butt, makes it awkward
jokes.explanation: Great callback that makes the previous joke even more uncomfortable by personalizing it

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You know. Whi... which reminds me. Nice to see you. Woo!

jokes.text: Well, another misguided, uh, Gray whale has turned up in Santa Monica bay, uh, recently. It was chased all the way through the L.A. River and right, uh, ultimately into o.j. Simpson's driveway.
jokes.setup: Story about a lost whale in Santa Monica Bay
jokes.punchline: Connects it to O.J. Simpson case (this is 1994-95, during the trial)
jokes.explanation: Good topical humor for the time - connects two unrelated news stories in an unexpected way

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, another misguided, uh, Gray whale has turned up in Santa Monica bay, uh, recently. It was chased all the way through the L.A. River and right, uh, ultimately into o.j. Simpson's driveway.

jokes.text: Please send all resumes to my producer Artie, and no sense of humor is required.
jokes.setup: Announcing head writer position opening
jokes.punchline: Self-deprecating joke that no sense of humor is required
jokes.explanation: Sharp self-referential humor about the show's writing quality - economical and character-appropriate

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Please send all resumes to my producer Artie, and no sense of humor is required.

jokes.text: Tom freeley... Tom freeley wouldn't know a joke if it drove at him and tried to park in the crack of his ass.
jokes.setup: Criticizing potential head writer candidates
jokes.punchline: Elaborate, crude metaphor about not recognizing jokes
jokes.explanation: Peak Artie - crude, elaborate, and oddly poetic insult that only his character could deliver

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Tom freeley... Tom freeley wouldn't know a joke if it drove at him and tried to park in the crack of his ass.

jokes.text: If she had a fondness for malt liquor, your niece could be my father-in-law.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Larry's 3-year-old niece's bad habits
jokes.punchline: Compares her to his alcoholic father-in-law
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie - dark family humor delivered matter-of-factly, economical and character-specific

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If she had a fondness for malt liquor, your niece could be my father-in-law.

jokes.text: A little late with Larry's melon, huh, beverly? Oh, well, I had to go and get a new one first, Arthur. I mean, I had it already cut up into little chunks the way he always likes it. Then he came in today, said he wanted it bald. I'd do it, except I'm already dating someone.
jokes.setup: Beverly explains Larry wanted his melon 'bald' instead of cut up
jokes.punchline: Phil misinterprets 'bald his melon' as sexual
jokes.explanation: Weak sexual innuendo - relies on misunderstanding rather than clever wordplay

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: A little late with Larry's melon, huh, beverly?

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Oh, well, I had to go and get a new one first, Arthur. I mean, I had it already cut up into little chunks the way he always likes it. Then he came in today, said he wanted it bald.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I'd do it, except I'm already dating someone.

jokes.text: If you're talking about balding Larry's melon, forget it, Phil. I didn't laugh the first time.
jokes.setup: Phil tries to continue the melon sexual joke
jokes.punchline: Artie shuts it down by saying he didn't find it funny the first time
jokes.explanation: Great meta-moment where Artie essentially critiques the previous weak joke within the show

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If you're talking about balding Larry's melon, forget it, Phil. I didn't laugh the first time.

jokes.text: Well, I charmed his ass. I told him roxanne was my favorite movie. I inquired about his play... in other words, you promised to blow him? Ho ho ho, Phil, you know all my little tricks, don't you?
jokes.setup: Paula explains how she got Steve Martin to agree to appear
jokes.punchline: Phil suggests she offered oral sex, Paula doesn't deny it
jokes.explanation: Standard Hollywood sex-for-favors joke - functional but not particularly original

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Well, I charmed his ass. I told him roxanne was my favorite movie. I inquired about his play...

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: in other words, you promised to blow him?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Ho ho ho, Phil, you know all my little tricks, don't you?

jokes.text: Well, it means, Phil, that you're a snide little prick. Not that we don't love that about you, but what we're looking for in a head writer is more experience, someone who is more of a people person.
jokes.setup: Artie explains why Phil isn't right for head writer position
jokes.punchline: Calls him a snide little prick but says they love that about him
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie brutal honesty - the fake compliment makes the insult even sharper

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, it means, Phil, that you're a snide little prick. Not that we don't love that about you, but what we're looking for in a head writer is more experience, someone who is more of a people person.

jokes.text: That's true. In the last 2 minutes alone, you offered to fuck Larry's melon and you accused Paula of promising to orally service a distinguished guest. Let's face it, son, you're a born diplomat.
jokes.setup: Phil claims he's a people person
jokes.punchline: Artie lists Phil's recent inappropriate comments, calls him a 'born diplomat' sarcastically
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that ties together Phil's inappropriate behavior with perfect sarcasm

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That's true. In the last 2 minutes alone, you offered to fuck Larry's melon and you accused Paula of promising to orally service a distinguished guest. Let's face it, son, you're a born diplomat.

jokes.text: Unless they found out who killed Bob eubanks' tree.
jokes.setup: Discussion about police being there for Hank
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous crime: killing Bob Eubanks' tree
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Hollywood reference - specific enough to feel real, ridiculous enough to be funny

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Unless they found out who killed Bob eubanks' tree.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Well, you got me. And I want to apologize. I just... um, it's about the, uh, handicap parking plates, is that right?
jokes.setup: Hank assumes he's in trouble with police
jokes.punchline: Immediately confesses to having fake handicap plates
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - immediately assumes guilt and confesses to wrong crime

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, my God. Well, you got me. And I want to apologize. I just... um, it's about the, uh, handicap parking plates, is that right?

jokes.text: I mean, I suspected some marijuana. You know, sure, you know, what we used to call wacky tobacky. Well, I still call it that... wacky weed.
jokes.setup: Hank discussing drug problem at his restaurant
jokes.punchline: Uses outdated slang terms for marijuana, corrects himself to another outdated term
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank character work - his attempt to sound hip by using outdated slang

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I mean, I suspected some marijuana. You know, sure, you know, what we used to call wacky tobacky. Well, I still call it that... wacky weed.

jokes.text: You know, I mean, pot smoking, I don't mind. The kids, you know, to get the munchies... hot wing sales go right through the roof. It's when these kids get on God-knows-what, you know, they eat like little birds.
jokes.setup: Hank explaining his drug policy at restaurant
jokes.punchline: He's okay with pot because it increases food sales, but harder drugs hurt business
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy - Hank's purely business-minded approach to drug policy is hilariously amoral

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You know, I mean, pot smoking, I don't mind. The kids, you know, to get the munchies... hot wing sales go right through the roof. It's when these kids get on God-knows-what, you know, they eat like little birds.

jokes.text: I mean, what... what... what if one of these poor kids, you know, gets on, uh, you know, some bad stuff? No one likes to eat at a restaurant where someone has died as a rule.
jokes.setup: Hank worried about drug overdoses at his restaurant
jokes.punchline: His concern is purely business-related - death is bad for restaurant atmosphere
jokes.explanation: Perfect continuation of Hank's amoral business logic - treats potential death as PR problem

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I mean, what... what... what if one of these poor kids, you know, gets on, uh, you know, some bad stuff? No one likes to eat at a restaurant where someone has died as a rule.

jokes.text: These are all gonna look like he's talking, I'm telling ya.
jokes.setup: Photography session continuing during serious conversation
jokes.punchline: Photographer obsessed with the talking photo problem despite context
jokes.explanation: Running gag that provides comedic relief during serious drug discussion

dialogue.speaker: Albert
dialogue.line: These are all gonna look like he's talking, I'm telling ya.

jokes.text: He gave it a great deal of thought. He feels it's the right way to go. Unbelievable. Thanks, Mike.
jokes.setup: Phil asks if Larry really thought about making him head writer
jokes.punchline: Phil thanks 'Mike' instead of Artie, showing he's not detail-oriented
jokes.explanation: Subtle character detail - Phil's mistake reveals he's not paying attention to names

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: He gave it a great deal of thought. He feels it's the right way to go.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Unbelievable. Thanks, Mike.

jokes.text: Just stay away from your usual attire, that's all. Hell, you look like you're a member of peanuts gang. Linus with goatee.
jokes.setup: Artie telling Phil to dress better for network meeting
jokes.punchline: Compares Phil's look to Linus from Peanuts with facial hair
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual insult - specific, unexpected, and perfectly captures Phil's appearance

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Just stay away from your usual attire, that's all. Hell, you look like you're a member of peanuts gang. Linus with goatee.

jokes.text: Whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, little big man. Steve Martin's not doing this sketch. He's just paneling. Well, that may have been the case, but, uh, I'd like you to send it to him.
jokes.setup: Paula calls Phil 'little big man' and explains Steve Martin situation
jokes.punchline: Phil insists on sending sketch despite being told it won't happen
jokes.explanation: Good power struggle comedy - Phil's new authority clashing with Paula's experience

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, little big man. Steve Martin's not doing this sketch. He's just paneling.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Well, that may have been the case, but, uh, I'd like you to send it to him.

jokes.text: Oh, goody! That means you'll be gone in a month.
jokes.setup: Phil insists he has authority as head writer
jokes.punchline: Paula sarcastically celebrates because head writers don't last long
jokes.explanation: Sharp callback to established show lore about head writer turnover - economical and cutting

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Oh, goody! That means you'll be gone in a month.

jokes.text: What is this? Youth. Heh heh.
jokes.setup: Phil continues to argue against network executive's judgment
jokes.punchline: Dennis dismisses Phil's persistence as mere youthful inexperience
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - one word plus chuckle completely deflates Phil's authority

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: What is this? Youth. Heh heh.

jokes.text: Are you insane? What? What are you thinking you're doing in here? I came prepared. You said that these meetings are very important. Yes, they are. All the more reason to shut the fuck up.
jokes.setup: Phil defends speaking up in network meeting because meetings are important
jokes.punchline: Artie's logic: meetings are important, therefore don't speak
jokes.explanation: Classic Hollywood paradox - important meetings require silence, delivered with Artie's brutal honesty

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Are you insane? What? What are you thinking you're doing in here? I came prepared. You said that these meetings are very important.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Yes, they are. All the more reason to shut the fuck up.

jokes.text: 'Not feasible at this juncture?' Phil, did that sentence come with the suit or did you have to buy it separately?
jokes.setup: Phil uses corporate jargon 'not feasible at this juncture'
jokes.punchline: Writer suggests the pretentious language came with Phil's new suit
jokes.explanation: Excellent metaphor connecting Phil's new clothes with his new corporate speak

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: 'Not feasible at this juncture?' Phil, did that sentence come with the suit or did you have to buy it separately?

jokes.text: Yeah, he got the fax, and he's got some problems. He thinks you're making fun of mute people. It is a takeoff on the piano. It does not work without a mute.
jokes.setup: Steve Martin objects to sketch because it makes fun of mute people
jokes.punchline: Phil's defense that the sketch requires a mute person to work
jokes.explanation: Shows Phil's obliviousness to sensitivity issues - his artistic defense misses the point

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Yeah, he got the fax, and he's got some problems. He thinks you're making fun of mute people.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: It is a takeoff on the piano. It does not work without a mute.

jokes.text: Well, thanks for your help, bitch. Oh, that'd be so much hotter if you were wearing your tie.
jokes.setup: Phil angrily calls Paula a bitch
jokes.punchline: Paula's sexual comeback about his corporate attire
jokes.explanation: Paula turns Phil's anger into sexual innuendo, maintaining her power in the dynamic

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Well, thanks for your help, bitch.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Oh, that'd be so much hotter if you were wearing your tie.

jokes.text: Larry drives through the canyons every day at 4:00 to unwind before the show. Then why did he agree to these meetings? When he agreed to meet with you, this is what he meant
jokes.setup: Phil expects to meet with Larry at 4:00 as promised
jokes.punchline: Larry 'meeting' means driving alone in canyons where he can't be reached
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry logic - agrees to meetings but makes them impossible, classic avoidance behavior

dialogue.line: Larry drives through the canyons every day at 4:00 to unwind before the show. Then why did he agree to these meetings? When he agreed to meet with you, this is what he meant

jokes.text: Car phones don't work in the canyons. Bingo. Got it?
jokes.setup: Phil suggests calling Larry on his car phone
jokes.punchline: Artie reveals Larry chose that route specifically because phones don't work there
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reveal - Larry's 'unwinding' is actually elaborate avoidance scheme, perfectly planned

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Car phones don't work in the canyons. Bingo. Got it?

jokes.text: Do you need an actual piano or can you use one of those casio keyboards? I need an ornate grand piano. Good. You aim for the grand. When you get the casio, be thrilled.
jokes.setup: Phil needs a grand piano for the sketch
jokes.punchline: Artie's realistic advice about lowered expectations in TV production
jokes.explanation: Classic show business reality check - Artie's experience vs Phil's naive expectations

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Do you need an actual piano or can you use one of those casio keyboards?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I need an ornate grand piano.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Good. You aim for the grand. When you get the casio, be thrilled.

jokes.text: Arthur, do you remember the bosnian sheepdog joke? Mike wrote that. It got a huge applause break. I'm sure he'll always cherish that moment.
jokes.setup: Phil defends writer Mike who's being fired
jokes.punchline: Artie dismissively says Mike will 'cherish that moment' - one joke in his career
jokes.explanation: Brutal dismissal of writer's career - reduces entire output to one memorable joke

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Arthur, do you remember the bosnian sheepdog joke? Mike wrote that. It got a huge applause break. I'm sure he'll always cherish that moment.

jokes.text: Guys, I'm trying to finish this, OK? That's what you should do. Just tell Larry he has to use some of the jokes from last week, 'cause that was funny stuff, Phil.
jokes.setup: Writers complaining about Larry rejecting good material
jokes.punchline: Suggestion that Phil just force Larry to use old jokes - shows misunderstanding of power dynamics
jokes.explanation: Shows writers' frustration but also their naive understanding of how the show works

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: Guys, I'm trying to finish this, OK? That's what you should do. Just tell Larry he has to use some of the jokes from last week, 'cause that was funny stuff, Phil.

jokes.text: Fine. Okay, but I can jerk off anywhere.
jokes.setup: Phil tells writers to jerk off in corner
jokes.punchline: Writer matter-of-factly accepts but notes location flexibility
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response - treats Phil's crude dismissal with practical consideration

dialogue.speaker: Writer
dialogue.line: Fine. Okay, but I can jerk off anywhere.

jokes.text: Go fuck yourself. Do my job? You do it, asswipe. You get on the phone and find a last-minute replacement for Steve Martin, someone willing to do a hack's sketch about the piano, which, by the way, is 6 months too late. Nice topical meter, dipshit.
jokes.setup: Phil and Paula's argument escalating
jokes.punchline: Paula's brutal critique of Phil's sketch as both hack work and outdated
jokes.explanation: Paula's professional critique disguised as personal attack - she's both angry and right about the sketch

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Go fuck yourself. Do my job? You do it, asswipe. You get on the phone and find a last-minute replacement for Steve Martin, someone willing to do a hack's sketch about the piano, which, by the way, is 6 months too late. Nice topical meter, dipshit.

jokes.text: I'm Dr. Wagner, and I want you both to know that, uh, I've been a marriage counselor for 10 years. And just by the fact that you're here now proves to me that you'd like to save your relationship.
jokes.setup: Larry playing marriage counselor in sketch
jokes.punchline: The setup for the sketch parody of 'The Piano'
jokes.explanation: Standard sketch setup - not particularly funny on its own, just establishing the premise

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm Dr. Wagner, and I want you both to know that, uh, I've been a marriage counselor for 10 years. And just by the fact that you're here now proves to me that you'd like to save your relationship.

jokes.text: Don't look at her. She's a mute. We've been married for 2 years.
jokes.setup: Marriage counseling session
jokes.punchline: Reveals wife is mute, which creates the sketch's central premise
jokes.explanation: Basic sketch premise setup - relies on 'The Piano' reference that audience may not get

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: Don't look at her. She's a mute. We've been married for 2 years.

jokes.text: Did you see the piano? Yes, I did, and I thought that you, uh, got along very well in that, if I may say. Oh, we did, but then she plays the same song over and over. At this point, chopsticks would be a thrill.
jokes.setup: Reference to 'The Piano' movie and the mute wife's piano playing
jokes.punchline: Husband's frustration with repetitive playing, chopsticks as improvement
jokes.explanation: Decent marital complaint joke - chopsticks reference works for those who get the movie parody

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Did you see the piano?

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: Yes, I did, and I thought that you, uh, got along very well in that, if I may say.

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: Oh, we did, but then she plays the same song over and over.

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: At this point, chopsticks would be a thrill.

jokes.text: Now, am I thinking of something else? But it seems to me in the piano, she started to learn to speak at the end. Oh, typical Hollywood ending. In reality, she's still a mute.
jokes.setup: Dr. Wagner references the movie ending where character learns to speak
jokes.punchline: Guest dismisses it as 'typical Hollywood ending' versus reality
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary on movie vs reality - works on multiple levels for different audiences

dialogue.speaker: Larry as Dr. Wagner
dialogue.line: Now, am I thinking of something else? But it seems to me in the piano, she started to learn to speak at the end.

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: Oh, typical Hollywood ending. In reality, she's still a mute.

jokes.text: Why don't you go screw yourself? Now... oh, oh, my God. You're an amazing therapist. This is the first time she's spoken.
jokes.setup: Mute wife suddenly speaks with an insult
jokes.punchline: Husband credits the therapist for breakthrough, misses that she told him to screw himself
jokes.explanation: Good sketch logic - husband so focused on breakthrough he ignores the hostile content

dialogue.speaker: Mute Wife
dialogue.line: Why don't you go screw yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: Now... oh, oh, my God. You're an amazing therapist. This is the first time she's spoken.

jokes.text: You know, you use me. You take me for granted. And... and what's with the marks on the face? I mean, let's talk... they're genuine maori tattoos, that's what they are. Eh, maori this, will you please?
jokes.setup: Wife complaining about husband's Maori tattoos
jokes.punchline: Husband's terrible pun 'maori this' dismissing her concerns
jokes.explanation: Weak pun that feels forced - shows sketch is running out of steam

dialogue.speaker: Mute Wife
dialogue.line: You know, you use me. You take me for granted. And... and what's with the marks on the face? I mean, let's talk... they're genuine maori tattoos, that's what they are.

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: Eh, maori this, will you please?

jokes.text: I can make her stop. That'll cost you an extra $1500. Can you see me at 5:00 next Tuesday? I can work you in between the rain man and the guy from my left foot.
jokes.setup: Husband desperate for therapist to make wife stop talking
jokes.punchline: Therapist charges extra and reveals other movie character patients
jokes.explanation: Good sketch ending - expands the premise to other movie characters, shows therapist as opportunist

dialogue.speaker: Larry as Dr. Wagner
dialogue.line: I can make her stop. That'll cost you an extra $1500. Can you see me at 5:00 next Tuesday? I can work you in between the rain man and the guy from my left foot.

jokes.text: You want Paula fired? Yes, and I want to watch while you do it.
jokes.setup: Phil listing his demands as head writer
jokes.punchline: Wants to watch Paula get fired - shows his vindictive nature
jokes.explanation: Shows Phil's power corruption - wants revenge and entertainment from firing

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: You want Paula fired? Yes, and I want to watch while you do it.

jokes.text: These have been the worst 4 days of my life. I have no more friends here, I'm the only one who does any of the work, and at the end of the day, all I can think about is how I have to come back here and do it all over again tomorrow. It's a nightmare.
jokes.setup: Phil's dramatic breakdown about being head writer
jokes.punchline: Complete reversal from wanting the job to calling it a nightmare
jokes.explanation: Perfect character arc completion - Phil learns harsh reality of management responsibility

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: These have been the worst 4 days of my life. I have no more friends here, I'm the only one who does any of the work, and at the end of the day, all I can think about is how I have to come back here and do it all over again tomorrow. It's a nightmare.

jokes.text: Lot more fun being the snotty little dork, wasn't it? Congratulations, son. You're the head writer. But I don't want the job. That's too damn bad.
jokes.setup: Phil wants to quit being head writer
jokes.punchline: Artie won't let him quit - forces him to keep the job he now hates
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal - Phil trapped in position he fought to get, Artie's cruel wisdom

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Lot more fun being the snotty little dork, wasn't it? Congratulations, son. You're the head writer.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: But I don't want the job.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That's too damn bad.

jokes.text: You're making 6500 bucks a week, sweetie. Doesn't that soften the blows a bit? 6500... no, no, no. I'm only making 37,500. Oh, damn it! There's been a horrible mistake. Jesus, I have to fix that right away.
jokes.setup: Artie mentions Phil's salary to console him
jokes.punchline: Phil's actual salary is much lower, Artie 'realizes' mistake (implies he's cutting Phil's pay)
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - appears Artie made error in Phil's favor, actually reveals he's about to cut his pay

dialogue.line: You're making 6500 bucks a week, sweetie. Doesn't that soften the blows a bit?

jokes.punchline: Artie's casual agreement to fire people - continuation of firing theme
jokes.explanation: Shows Larry's casual attitude toward firing people - caps off episode's theme of workplace cruelty

dialogue.line: Listen, I'm also having a problem with kelly the prop girl. I'd like to replace her.
